questions.specification: A discrete random variable $X$ follows a Poisson distribution $\text{Po}(\mu)$.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Statistics and Probability > AHL 4.17—Poisson distribution > Question Type 2: Finding the probability of some event given some contextual information of a poisson distribution
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 2: Finding the probability of some event given some contextual information of a poisson distribution
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question is entirely about Poisson distribution properties: deriving the recursive relationship for the pmf P(X=x+1) in terms of P(X=x), then using given Poisson probabilities to solve for the parameter μ.

parts.content: (a) Show that $\text{P}(X = x + 1) = \frac{\mu}{x + 1} \times \text{P}(X = x), \quad x \in \mathbb{N}$.
parts.markscheme:
METHOD 1

  • State definition of $\text{P}(X = x + 1)$: $\text{P}(X = x + 1) = \frac{\mu^{x + 1}}{(x + 1)!}e^{-\mu}$ A1
  • Factor expression: $= \frac{\mu}{x + 1} \times \frac{\mu^x}{x!}e^{-\mu}$ M1A1
  • Substitute $\text{P}(X = x)$: $= \frac{\mu}{x + 1} \times \text{P}(X = x)$ AG

METHOD 2

  • Substitute expression for $\text{P}(X = x)$: $\frac{\mu}{x + 1} \times \text{P}(X = x) = \frac{\mu}{x + 1} \times \frac{\mu^x}{x!}e^{-\mu}$ A1
  • Combine terms: $= \frac{\mu^{x + 1}}{(x + 1)!}e^{-\mu}$ M1A1
  • Identify definition of $\text{P}(X = x + 1)$: $= \text{P}(X = x + 1)$ AG

METHOD 3

  • Form ratio: $\frac{\text{P}(X = x + 1)}{\text{P}(X = x)} = \frac{\frac{\mu^{x + 1}}{(x + 1)!}e^{-\mu}}{\frac{\mu^x}{x!}e^{-\mu}}$ M1
  • Simplify expression: $= \frac{\mu^{x + 1}}{\mu^x} \times \frac{x!}{(x + 1)!}$ A1
  • Obtain result: $= \frac{\mu}{x + 1}$ A1
  • Conclude relationship: $\text{P}(X = x + 1) = \frac{\mu}{x + 1} \times \text{P}(X = x)$ AG

3 marks total

steps.title: State the Poisson formula
steps.content:
To solve this, we start with the definition of the probability mass function (PMF) for a Poisson distribution. If $X \sim \text{Po}(\mu)$, then for any non-negative integer $k$:

$$P(X = k) = \frac{\mu^k e^{-\mu}}{k!}$$

This formula is the foundation for our derivation.

steps.title: Write the expression for LHS
steps.content:
We begin with the left-hand side (LHS) of the equation we want to show. We substitute $k = x + 1$ into our Poisson formula:

$$P(X = x + 1) = \frac{\mu^{x + 1}}{(x + 1)!} e^{-\mu}$$

steps.title: Apply algebraic factoring
steps.content:
Now, we use two algebraic properties to break down the terms in the numerator and denominator:

1.  **Exponents**: $\mu^{x+1} = \mu \times \mu^x$
2.  **Factorials**: $(x+1)! = (x+1) \times x!$

Substituting these into our expression, we get:

$$P(X = x + 1) = \frac{\mu \times \mu^x}{(x + 1) \times x!} e^{-\mu}$$

steps.title: Isolate the coefficient
steps.content:
We can now rearrange the factors to separate the term $\frac{\mu}{x+1}$ from the rest of the expression:

$$P(X = x + 1) = \frac{\mu}{x + 1} \times \left( \frac{\mu^x e^{-\mu}}{x!} \right)$$

steps.title: Identify and substitute P(X=x)
steps.content:
Notice that the expression inside the parentheses is exactly the definition of $P(X = x)$. 

$$\frac{\mu^x e^{-\mu}}{x!} = P(X = x)$$

Substituting this back into our equation gives the final result:

$$P(X = x + 1) = \frac{\mu}{x + 1} \times P(X = x)$$ 

This confirms the relationship required by the question.

steps.title: Recall Poisson PMF
steps.content:
To solve this problem, we first need the definition of the Poisson probability mass function (PMF). For a random variable $X$ following a Poisson distribution with mean $\mu$, the probability of getting exactly $x$ occurrences is:

$$\text{P}(X = x) = \frac{\mu^x e^{-\mu}}{x!}$$

This is a fundamental formula found in **Topic 4: Statistics and Probability** of your syllabus.

steps.title: Substitute into the RHS
steps.content:
Using the **Substitution into RHS** method, we start with the right-hand side of the identity we want to prove and substitute the formula for $\text{P}(X = x)$:

$$\text{RHS} = \frac{\mu}{x + 1} \times \text{P}(X = x)$$

Substituting our PMF formula:

$$\text{RHS} = \frac{\mu}{x + 1} \times \frac{\mu^x e^{-\mu}}{x!}$$

steps.title: Simplify using algebraic rules
steps.content:
Now we simplify the expression by combining terms. 

1. In the numerator, we combine the powers of $\mu$: $\mu^1 \times \mu^x = \mu^{x+1}$.
2. In the denominator, we use the property of factorials where $(x + 1) \times x! = (x + 1)!$.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{RHS} &= \frac{\mu \times \mu^x e^{-\mu}}{(x + 1) \times x!} \\ &= \frac{\mu^{x+1} e^{-\mu}}{(x + 1)!} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Identify the result
steps.content:
Finally, we compare our simplified expression to the definition of $\text{P}(X = x + 1)$. 

By substituting $x + 1$ into the original Poisson formula, we get exactly:

$$\text{P}(X = x + 1) = \frac{\mu^{x+1} e^{-\mu}}{(x + 1)!}$$

Since our simplified RHS matches this perfectly, we have shown that:

$$\text{P}(X = x + 1) = \frac{\mu}{x + 1} \times \text{P}(X = x)$$

steps.title: Define the Poisson PMF
steps.content:
To solve this using the **Ratio of Probabilities** approach, we first need the formula for the probability mass function (PMF) of a Poisson distribution. This is a fundamental concept in **AHL 4.17: Poisson distribution**.

For a random variable $X \sim \text{Po}(\mu)$, the probability that $X$ takes the value $x$ is given by:

$$\text{P}(X = x) = \frac{\mu^x e^{-\mu}}{x!}$$

steps.title: Set up the ratio
steps.content:
The question asks us to show a relationship between $\text{P}(X = x + 1)$ and $\text{P}(X = x)$. Let's start by forming the ratio of these two probabilities:

$$\frac{\text{P}(X = x + 1)}{\text{P}(X = x)} = \frac{\frac{\mu^{x+1} e^{-\mu}}{(x+1)!}}{\frac{\mu^x e^{-\mu}}{x!}}$$

steps.title: Simplify common terms
steps.content:
Now we simplify the fraction. First, notice that $e^{-\mu}$ appears in both the numerator and the denominator, so it cancels out. We can also group the $\mu$ terms and the factorials together:

$$\frac{\text{P}(X = x + 1)}{\text{P}(X = x)} = \frac{\mu^{x+1}}{\mu^x} \times \frac{x!}{(x+1)!}$$

steps.title: Simplify exponents and factorials
steps.content:
Next, we apply the laws of exponents and the property of factorials. 

Recall that:
1. $\frac{\mu^{x+1}}{\mu^x} = \mu^{x+1-x} = \mu^1 = \mu$
2. $(x+1)! = (x+1) \times x!$

Substituting these into our expression:

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\text{P}(X = x + 1)}{\text{P}(X = x)} &= \mu \times \frac{x!}{(x+1)x!} \\ &= \frac{\mu}{x+1} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Conclude the proof
steps.content:
The final step is to multiply both sides of the equation by $\text{P}(X = x)$ to reach the identity required by the question:

$$\text{P}(X = x + 1) = \frac{\mu}{x + 1} \times \text{P}(X = x)$$ 

This completes the proof.

parts.content: (b) Given that $\text{P}(X = 2) = 0.241667$ and $\text{P}(X = 3) = 0.112777$, use part (a) to find the value of $\mu$.
parts.markscheme:
• Substitute values into formula from part (a): $\text{P}(X = 3) = \frac{\mu}{3} \times \text{P}(X = 2) \implies 0.112777 = \frac{\mu}{3} \times 0.241667$ A1
  • Attempt to solve for $\mu$ M1
  • Calculate $\mu$: $\mu = 1.40$ A1

3 marks total

steps.title: Recall the recurrence relation
steps.content:
From part (a), we know that for a Poisson distribution with mean $\mu$, the probabilities of consecutive values are related by a recurrence formula. Specifically, for $x=3$, the relationship is:

$$\text{P}(X = 3) = \frac{\mu}{3} \times \text{P}(X = 2)$$

This formula allows us to find $\mu$ by simply plugging in the probabilities we already have.

steps.title: Substitute the known values
steps.content:
The question provides the numerical values for both probabilities. We can substitute these directly into our formula:

$$0.112777 = \frac{\mu}{3} \times 0.241667$$

This gives us a linear equation where $\mu$ is the only unknown variable.

steps.title: Isolate the variable μ
steps.content:
To solve for $\mu$, we need to rearrange the equation. We can do this by multiplying both sides by $3$ and then dividing by $0.241667$:

$$\begin{aligned} \mu &= \frac{3 \times 0.112777}{0.241667} \end{aligned}$$

This is the step where we prepare the expression for our calculator.

steps.title: Calculate the final value
steps.content:
Now, perform the calculation. 

$$\mu = 1.399988...$$

Rounding to three significant figures (the standard for IB), we get:

$$\mu = 1.40$$

gdc.instructions: In the Run-Matrix menu, input the fraction (3 × 0.112777) / 0.241667 and press EXE.

ti84.instructions: Enter (3 * 0.112777) / 0.241667 on the main screen and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into the input bar to see the result.

calculator.description: Calculate the value of mu using the rearranged expression.

parts.content: Find the probability of at least 2 defects in an hour.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify the distribution as $X \sim \operatorname{Po}(2.3)$ 
- Calculate the probability of at least 2 defects using the complement: $1 - \mathrm{P}(X \leq 1)$ <marks>M1</marks>
- State the final probability: $1 - (e^{-2.3} + 2.3e^{-2.3}) = 1 - 0.3308\dots = 0.669$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Define the distribution
steps.content:
The question specifies that the number of defects follows a Poisson distribution. We can let $X$ represent the number of defects per hour. The mean rate is given as 2.3 defects per hour.

$$X \sim \operatorname{Po}(2.3)$$ 

This means our parameter $m = 2.3$.

highlights.text: Poisson distribution with mean 2.3 per hour

steps.title: Use the complement rule
steps.content:
We need to find the probability of having at least 2 defects, which is $P(X \geq 2)$. 

Because the Poisson distribution can theoretically take any integer value from 0 to infinity, it is much easier to calculate the complement. The complement of "at least 2" is "0 or 1".

$$\begin{aligned} P(X \geq 2) &= 1 - P(X < 2) \\ &= 1 - [P(X=0) + P(X=1)] \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Apply probability formula
steps.content:
To calculate the individual probabilities, we use the Poisson probability formula from the IB Data Booklet:

$$P(X=x) = \frac{e^{-m} m^x}{x!}$$

Substitute $m = 2.3$ for $x = 0$ and $x = 1$:

$$\begin{aligned} P(X=0) &= \frac{e^{-2.3} \cdot 2.3^0}{0!} = e^{-2.3} \\ P(X=1) &= \frac{e^{-2.3} \cdot 2.3^1}{1!} = 2.3 \cdot e^{-2.3} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate final value
steps.content:
Now, we combine these values and subtract from 1:

$$\begin{aligned} P(X \geq 2) &= 1 - (e^{-2.3} + 2.3e^{-2.3}) \\ &\approx 1 - (0.10025... + 0.23059...) \\ &= 1 - 0.33085... \\ &= 0.66914... \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to 3 significant figures, we get $0.669$.

gdc.instructions: Use the Poisson Cumulative Distribution function (often denoted as Ppd or PoissonCD) with mean 2.3 and upper bound 1, then subtract from 1.

ti84.instructions: Go to 2nd > DISTR > select poissoncdf(. Enter λ: 2.3, x value: 1. Then calculate 1 minus this result.

desmos.instructions: Type the Poisson distribution function, set the cumulative bounds from 2 to infinity.

calculator.description: Calculate the probability of at least 2 defects by subtracting the sum of P(X=0) and P(X=1) from 1.

steps.title: Identify the distribution
steps.content:
The question states that the number of defects follows a Poisson distribution with a mean rate of 2.3 per hour. We can define our random variable $X$ as the number of defects in one hour:

$$X \sim \operatorname{Po}(2.3)$$

This falls under **Topic 4: Statistics and Probability** in your syllabus (specifically AHL 4.17).

highlights.text: Poisson distribution with mean 2.3 per hour

steps.title: Define the probability needed
steps.content:
We need to find the probability of having **at least 2** defects. In mathematical terms, this is:

$$P(X \geq 2)$$

Since the Poisson distribution can theoretically go up to infinity, we cannot calculate every individual probability from 2 onwards. Instead, we use the **complement rule**.

steps.title: Apply the complement rule
steps.content:
The total probability of all possible outcomes is always 1. The opposite (complement) of "at least 2" ($2, 3, 4, \dots$) is "fewer than 2" ($0$ or $1$).

Therefore:
$$P(X \geq 2) = 1 - P(X \leq 1)$$

This step is crucial for earning the first mark (M1) on the exam.

steps.title: Use the GDC PoissonCD
steps.content: Now we use the Graphing Display Calculator (GDC) to find the cumulative probability $P(X \leq 1)$. We use the **Poisson Cumulative Distribution Function (Poisson CDF)** because we are looking for the sum of probabilities from $X = 0$ up to $X = 1$.

gdc.instructions: Go to Statistics -> Dist -> Poisson -> Pcd. Set Data to 'Variable'. Enter x: 1 and λ: 2.3.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [VARS] (DISTR), scroll down to 'poissoncdf('. Enter mean (λ): 2.3 and x value: 1.

desmos.instructions: Type 'poisson(2.3)' and then use the '.cdf(0, 1)' function.

calculator.description: Calculate the cumulative Poisson probability for mean 2.3 up to x=1.

steps.title: Final calculation
steps.content:
Using the calculator, we find:
$$P(X \leq 1) \approx 0.330853...$$

Now, subtract this from 1 to find the final answer:
$$\begin{aligned} P(X \geq 2) &= 1 - 0.330853... \\ &\approx 0.669146... \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to **3 significant figures**, as required by IB standards, we get **0.669**.

gdc.instructions: Calculate 1 - 0.330853 in the main calculation menu.

ti84.instructions: Perform 1 minus the previous result.

desmos.instructions: Subtract the CDF value from 1.

calculator.description: Verify the final subtraction

parts.content: Over a 5-hour period, calculate the probability of exactly 10 defects.
parts.markscheme:
- Determine the mean for a 5-hour period: $\lambda = 5 \times 2.3 = 11.5$ <marks>M1</marks>
- State the expression for the Poisson probability: $\mathrm{P}(Y=10) = \frac{e^{-11.5} \cdot 11.5^{10}}{10!}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate the final probability: $0.113$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Adjust the mean
steps.content:
In a Poisson distribution, the mean $(\lambda)$ must match the specific time interval we are looking at. The question provides a rate per hour, but asks for a probability over 5 hours. 

We calculate the adjusted mean by multiplying the hourly rate by the number of hours:

$$\lambda = 2.3 \times 5 = 11.5$$

steps.title: Identify the Poisson formula
steps.content:
To find the probability of exactly $x$ occurrences, we use the Poisson probability mass function formula. While students often use calculator functions like `Poissonpdf`, the formula is a fundamental definition in **Topic 4.17: Poisson distribution**:

$$\mathrm{P}(X = x) = \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^x}{x!}$$

Where:
- $\lambda$ is the mean for the period ($11.5$)
- $x$ is the number of defects we want to find ($10$)
- $e$ is Euler's constant $(\approx 2.718)$

steps.title: Substitute and solve
steps.content:
Now, substitute our values into the formula to find the probability of exactly 10 defects:

$$\mathrm{P}(X = 10) = \frac{e^{-11.5} \times 11.5^{10}}{10!}$$

By calculating this expression, we get:

$$\mathrm{P}(X = 10) \approx 0.113$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the formula directly on the calculation screen or use the built-in Poisson probability function often found under 'Distribution' settings.

ti84.instructions: Go to the home screen and enter the expression using the 'e' and '!' (factorial) buttons. Note: For the factorial, press [MATH] -> [PROB] -> [4:!]. Alternatively, use [2nd] [VARS] (DISTR) and select B: poissonpdf(11.5, 10).

desmos.instructions: Type the formula directly into an expression line.

calculator.description: Evaluate the Poisson formula numerically.

steps.title: Final answer
steps.content:
The probability of detecting exactly 10 defects in a 5-hour period is **0.113** (to 3 significant figures).

In an IB exam, you earn marks for:
1. Finding the correct adjusted mean (11.5).
2. Showing the correct substitution into the Poisson formula.
3. Providing the final answer rounded correctly.

steps.title: Adjust the mean rate
steps.content:
The question gives us a rate of defects per hour, but we need to find the probability over a 5-hour period. Since the Poisson mean is proportional to the size of the interval, we multiply the hourly mean by the number of hours.

$$\lambda = 2.3 \times 5 = 11.5$$

This represents the expected number of defects over the 5-hour period.

steps.title: Define the distribution
steps.content:
Let $Y$ be the number of defects in a 5-hour period. Based on our calculation, $Y$ follows a Poisson distribution with mean $\lambda = 11.5$:

$$Y \sim \text{Po}(11.5)$$

We want to find the probability of having exactly 10 defects, which is written as $P(Y = 10)$.

steps.title: Use GDC for calculation
steps.content:
To find the probability of an exact value in a Poisson distribution, we use the **Poisson Probability Density Function (PoissonPdf)** on the calculator. 

We enter the mean ($\lambda = 11.5$) and the specific value we are looking for ($x = 10$).

gdc.instructions: Go to the Statistics menu, select Distributions, then Poisson, and choose 'Poisson P.D.'. Set 'x' to 10 and 'λ' to 11.5.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [VARS] (DISTR). Scroll down to 'PoissonPdf' and press [ENTER]. Set 'λ' to 11.5 and 'x value' to 10. Select 'Paste' and press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Type 'poisson(11.5)' in a new line. Click the 'find cumulative probability' button (though we want pdf, many online tools use this syntax). More accurately, type 'poisson(11.5).pdf(10)'.

calculator.description: Calculate the Poisson probability for exactly 10 occurrences with a mean of 11.5.

steps.title: State final probability
steps.content:
The calculator gives a value of approximately $0.112935...$. Rounding this to the standard 3 significant figures required by the IB:

$$P(Y = 10) \approx 0.113$$

parts.content: Determine the expected number of hours in an 8-hour shift with no defects.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate the probability of no defects in one hour: $\mathrm{P}(X=0) = e^{-2.3} \approx 0.10026$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Apply the formula for expectation in a binomial context or direct multiplication: $8 \times \mathrm{P}(X=0)$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate the final expected value: $8 \times 0.10026\dots = 0.802$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Define the probability of success
steps.content:
To find the expected number of hours with no defects, we first need to determine the probability that a single hour has zero defects. We are given that defects follow a Poisson distribution with a mean rate of 2.3 per hour.

Let $X$ be the number of defects in one hour, so $X \sim \text{Po}(2.3)$. We want to find $P(X = 0)$.

steps.title: Calculate zero-defect probability
steps.content:
The probability formula for a Poisson distribution is $P(X = x) = \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^x}{x!}$. 

For zero defects ($x = 0$), the formula simplifies to:

$$P(X = 0) = e^{-2.3}$$

Using a calculator, we find:

$$P(X = 0) \approx 0.100258...$$

gdc.instructions: Use the Poisson probability density function (Poisson PDF) with mean 2.3 and x = 0.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [LN] to get 'e^', then type -2.3 and press [ENTER]. Alternatively, use [2nd] [VARS] (DISTR) and select 'poissonpdf(', then enter 2.3 for λ and 0 for x.

desmos.instructions: Type e^(-2.3) into a cell or use the poisson(2.3) distribution function.

calculator.description: Calculate the probability of zero defects in one hour using the exponential function.

steps.title: Apply binomial expectation
steps.content:
We can think of each hour in the 8-hour shift as an independent trial. If an hour has no defects, we count it as a "success."

Let $Y$ be the number of successful hours in the shift. Since there are $n = 8$ hours and the probability of success in each is $p = P(X = 0)$, $Y$ follows a Binomial distribution: $Y \sim B(8, 0.100258...)$.

The expected number of successes is given by the formula:

$$E(Y) = np$$

steps.title: Calculate the final answer
steps.content:
Now, substitute the values into the expectation formula:

$$\begin{aligned} E(Y) &= 8 \times 0.100258... \\ E(Y) &\approx 0.80207... \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to three significant figures, the expected number of hours with no defects is $0.802$.

gdc.instructions: Multiply 8 by the previously calculated probability value.

ti84.instructions: Type 8 * 0.10025884 and press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Type 8 * 0.10025884 into the calculator cell.

calculator.description: Multiply the number of hours (8) by the probability of no defects (0.100258...).

steps.title: Define the hourly distribution
steps.content: First, we identify the distribution for a single hour. The question states that the number of defects follows a **Poisson distribution** with a mean rate $(\lambda)$ of $2.3$ per hour. To find the expected number of "defect-free" hours in a shift, we first need the probability that any given hour has zero defects.

highlights.text: Poisson distribution with mean 2.3 per hour

steps.title: Calculate probability using GDC
steps.content:
Using the **PoissonPDF** function on your graphing calculator, we can find the probability of exactly $0$ defects occurring in one hour, $P(X=0)$. 

We enter the mean $\lambda = 2.3$ and the value $x = 0$:

$$P(X=0) \approx 0.100258...$$

gdc.instructions: Go to the Statistics/Probability menu, select Distributions, then Poisson PD. Input mean (μ or λ) as 2.3 and x as 0.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [VARS] (DISTR), scroll down to 'poissonpdf(', press [ENTER]. Set λ: 2.3, x value: 0. Select 'Paste' and [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Type 'poisson(2.3)' to create the distribution, then find the probability at 0.

calculator.description: Calculate the probability of zero defects using the Poisson probability density function.

steps.title: Find the expected value
steps.content:
Now that we have the probability $(p)$ of an hour having no defects, we treat each hour in the $8$-hour shift as a trial. To find the expected number of hours, we multiply the total number of hours $(n = 8)$ by the probability of success $(p)$.

Using the formula for expectation:
$$\mathrm{E}(X) = n \times p$$

$$\text{Expected Hours} = 8 \times 0.100258...$$
$$\text{Expected Hours} \approx 0.802$$

gdc.instructions: Multiply the probability by 8.

ti84.instructions: Multiply the previous result by 8.

desmos.instructions: Multiply the Poisson result by 8.

calculator.description: Multiply the hourly probability by the 8-hour shift duration.

highlights.text: expected number of hours in an 8-hour shift with no defects

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Rounding our result to three significant figures, the expected number of hours with no defects is **0.802**.

This makes sense intuitively: because the mean is $2.3$ defects per hour, it is relatively unlikely for an hour to have zero defects, so we expect less than one full hour in the shift to be defect-free.

parts.content: If two independent production lines each have a Poisson mean of 2.3 defects per hour, find the probability that the total number of defects in one hour is at least 8, using a normal approximation. Include a sketch.
parts.markscheme:
- State the total mean for the sum of two independent Poisson variables: $\mu = 2.3 + 2.3 = 4.6$ <marks>M1</marks>
- State the normal approximation: $T \sim \mathrm{N}(4.6, 4.6)$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Apply the continuity correction for $\mathrm{P}(T \geq 8)$ to get $\mathrm{P}(T_{\text{norm}} > 7.5)$ and calculate the $z$-score: $z = \frac{7.5 - 4.6}{\sqrt{4.6}} \approx 1.352$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Find the probability: $\mathrm{P}(Z > 1.352) = 0.0882$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Sketch the normal curve showing the mean of 4.6 and the shaded area for $x > 7.5$ <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Find the probability that Lucca eats at least one banana in a particular day.
parts.markscheme:
• Let $X$ be the number of bananas eaten in one day
  • $X \sim \text{Po}(0.2)$
  • $\text{P}(X \geqslant 1) = 1 - \text{P}(X = 0)$ M1
  • $= 0.181 \, (= 1 - e^{-0.2})$ A1
2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the distribution
steps.content:
First, we identify that the number of bananas eaten, $X$, follows a Poisson distribution. The question provides the mean rate $\lambda$, which is necessary for our calculations.

$$X \sim \text{Po}(0.2)$$
$$\lambda = 0.2$$

steps.title: Use complementary probability
steps.content:
We need to find the probability that Lucca eats **at least one** banana, written as $P(X \geq 1)$. 

In probability, the sum of all possible outcomes is $1$. The complement of "at least one" is "zero". Therefore, it is much easier to calculate the probability of him eating no bananas and subtracting that from $1$:

$$P(X \geq 1) = 1 - P(X = 0)$$

steps.title: Apply the Poisson formula
steps.content:
To find $P(X = 0)$, we use the Poisson probability formula:

$$P(X = x) = \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^x}{x!}$$

Substituting $x = 0$ and $\lambda = 0.2$:

$$\begin{aligned} P(X = 0) &= \frac{e^{-0.2} \cdot 0.2^0}{0!} \\ &= e^{-0.2} \end{aligned}$$

*(Note: Any number to the power of $0$ is $1$, and $0! = 1$)*

steps.title: Calculate the final probability
steps.content:
Now we substitute our value for $P(X=0)$ back into our complementary equation and solve:

$$\begin{aligned} P(X \geq 1) &= 1 - e^{-0.2} \\ &\approx 1 - 0.8187\dots \\ &= 0.181 \end{aligned}$$

Following IB standard practice, we provide the final answer to **3 significant figures**.

gdc.instructions: Type 1 - exp(-0.2) and press EXE/ENTER

calculator.description: Calculate the final probability directly using the exponential function.

steps.title: Define the distribution
steps.content:
First, we identify that the number of bananas eaten, $X$, follows a Poisson distribution. The question provides the mean ($\mu$ or $\lambda$), which is the only parameter we need for this distribution.

$$X \sim \text{Po}(0.2)$$

This topic is part of **AHL 4.17: Poisson distribution** in your syllabus.

steps.title: Identify the required probability
steps.content:
We are asked to find the probability that Lucca eats **at least one** banana. In mathematical terms, this means we want to find:

$$P(X \geq 1)$$

Since a Poisson distribution can theoretically go up to infinity, we use the cumulative distribution function (CDF) on our calculator to find the sum of probabilities for $X = 1, 2, 3, \dots$.

steps.title: Use GDC Poisson CDF
steps.content: Using the Poisson CDF function on your graphing calculator is the most efficient way to solve this. We set the mean to $0.2$ and calculate the probability for the range where $X$ is at least $1$.

gdc.instructions: Go to the Distribution menu, select Poisson, then Cumulative Distribution (CD). Set the Lower bound to 1, the Upper bound to a very large number (e.g., 999), and the mean (μ) to 0.2.

ti84.instructions: Since the TI-84 Poisson CDF calculates P(X ≤ x), use the complement: 1 - P(X = 0). Press [2nd] [VARS] (DISTR), scroll to 'poissoncdf(', enter Mean: 0.2, x value: 0. Then subtract the result from 1.

desmos.instructions: Type 'poisson(0.2)' into a cell. Click the 'Find Cumulative Probability (CDF)' checkbox. Set the Min to 1 and leave the Max as infinity.

calculator.description: Calculate P(X ≥ 1) for a Poisson distribution with mean 0.2.

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
After performing the calculation on the GDC, we get a value of approximately $0.181269\dots$

Following the IB standard of rounding to 3 significant figures, we get:

$$P(X \geq 1) \approx 0.181$$

parts.content: Find the expected number of weeks in the year in which Lucca eats no bananas.
parts.markscheme:
EITHER

  • Let $Y$ be the number of bananas eaten in one week
  • $Y \sim \text{Po}(1.4)$ A1
  • $\text{P}(Y = 0) = 0.246596\ldots \, (= e^{-1.4})$ A1

OR

  • Let $Z$ be the number of days in one week where Lucca eats at least one banana
  • $Z \sim \text{B}(7, 0.181\ldots)$ A1
  • $\text{P}(Z = 0) = 0.246596\ldots$ A1

THEN

  • Calculate expected number of weeks: $52 \times 0.246596\ldots$ M1
  • $= 12.8 \, (= 52e^{-1.4})$ A1
4 marks total

steps.title: Calculate the weekly mean
steps.content:
Since we are looking for the number of **weeks** in a year, we first need to find the mean number of bananas Lucca eats per week. We are given that he eats a mean of $0.2$ bananas per day. Because Poisson distributions scale linearly over time, we multiply the daily mean by $7$:

$$\lambda_{\text{week}} = 0.2 \times 7 = 1.4$$

gdc.instructions: Enter 0.2 * 7.

ti84.instructions: Enter 0.2 * 7 on the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Type 0.2 * 7.

calculator.description: Calculate the weekly mean.

steps.title: Probability of zero bananas
steps.content:
Next, we find the probability that Lucca eats **no** bananas ($x = 0$) in a given week. Using the Poisson probability formula $P(X = x) = \frac{e^{-m} m^x}{x!}$ with $m = 1.4$:

$$\begin{aligned} P(Y = 0) &= \frac{e^{-1.4} \times 1.4^0}{0!} \\ &= e^{-1.4} \\ &\approx 0.246597\dots \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Use the Poisson probability density function with mean 1.4 and x = 0.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [VARS] (DISTR), select 'poissonpdf(', enter 1.4 for λ and 0 for x. Alternatively, type e^(-1.4).

calculator.description: Calculate the probability using the exponential function or the Poisson PDF tool.

steps.title: Expected number of weeks
steps.content:
Now we treat each week as a trial. There are $52$ weeks in a year. The expected value for a binomial scenario (where a week either has zero bananas or doesn't) is given by $E(X) = np$ in the **Statistics and Probability** section of your data booklet.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Expected weeks} &= 52 \times P(Y = 0) \\ &= 52 \times 0.246597\dots \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Multiply 52 by the probability.

ti84.instructions: Enter 52 * Ans (using the previous result) or 52 * 0.246597.

calculator.description: Multiply the number of weeks by the probability found in the previous step.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Calculating the final value:

$$12.823\dots \approx 12.8 \text{ weeks}$$

In the IB exam, remember to round your final answer to **3 significant figures** unless stated otherwise.

steps.title: Find daily zero probability
steps.content:
To find the probability that Lucca eats no bananas in a single week using the **Daily Probability Approach**, we start by looking at one day. 

The number of bananas eaten per day, $X$, follows a Poisson distribution with mean $\lambda = 0.2$. The formula for the Poisson probability is:

$$P(X = x) = \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^x}{x!}$$

For $x = 0$ bananas in one day:

$$P(X = 0) = \frac{e^{-0.2} \cdot 0.2^0}{0!} = e^{-0.2}$$

steps.title: Calculate weekly zero probability
steps.content:
A week consists of 7 independent days. For Lucca to eat no bananas in a whole week, he must eat zero bananas on Monday AND zero on Tuesday, and so on, for all 7 days.

We calculate this by raising the daily probability to the power of 7:

$$P(\text{no bananas in a week}) = (e^{-0.2})^7 = e^{-1.4}$$

Using a calculator, this value is approximately:

$$P \approx 0.246596\dots$$

gdc.instructions: Use the exponential function to calculate e raised to the power of -1.4.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [e^x], type -1.4, and press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Type e^(-1.4) into the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the probability of zero bananas over 7 days.

steps.title: Determine the expected value
steps.content:
Now we need to find the expected number of weeks in a year ($n = 52$) that this event occurs. 

The expected value $E(X)$ for a discrete distribution is given by the formula:

$$E(X) = n \times p$$

Where:
- $n = 52$ (number of weeks in a year)
- $p = e^{-1.4}$ (probability of no bananas in a week)

$$\begin{aligned} E &= 52 \times 0.246596\dots \\ &\approx 12.8 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Multiply 52 by the previously calculated probability.

ti84.instructions: Type 52 * 0.24659696 and press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Type 52 * 0.24659696 into the expression bar.

calculator.description: Multiply the weekly probability by the number of weeks in a year.

steps.title: State the final result
steps.content:
Performing the final calculation:

$$52 \times 0.246596\dots \approx 12.823\dots$$

Rounding to three significant figures (as is standard in IB exams unless stated otherwise), we get:

**Expected number of weeks = 12.8**

parts.content: Find the probability that no events occur in a unit interval.
parts.markscheme:
Let $X \sim \operatorname{Po}(5.4)$.
$\mathrm{P}(X=0)=e^{-5.4} \approx 0.00452$ 
<marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the distribution
steps.content:
First, we identify that the number of events $X$ follows a Poisson distribution. The question provides the mean rate for a unit interval, which we denote as $\lambda$.

$$X \sim \operatorname{Po}(5.4)$$

Here, $\lambda = 5.4$ and we want to find the probability of having **no events**, which means $k = 0$.

steps.title: State the Poisson formula
steps.content:
To calculate the probability of exactly $k$ events occurring, we use the Poisson probability mass function formula:

$$P(X = k) = \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^k}{k!}$$

This formula is the fundamental definition used in **Topic 4.17: Poisson distribution**.

steps.title: Substitute and simplify
steps.content:
Now, we substitute $\lambda = 5.4$ and $k = 0$ into the formula:

$$P(X = 0) = \frac{e^{-5.4} \times 5.4^0}{0!}$$

Since any number to the power of $0$ is $1$ ($5.4^0 = 1$) and $0! = 1$, the expression simplifies significantly:

$$P(X = 0) = e^{-5.4}$$

steps.title: Calculate the final answer
steps.content:
Finally, we evaluate $e^{-5.4}$ using a calculator. 

$$P(X = 0) \approx 0.00451658...$$

Rounding to the standard IB requirement of **3 significant figures**, we get:

$$P(X = 0) \approx 0.00452$$

gdc.instructions: Enter exp(-5.4) and press execute.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [e^x], enter -5.4, and press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Type e^(-5.4) in the expression bar.

steps.title: Define the distribution
steps.content:
The question states that the number of events follows a Poisson distribution with a mean of 5.4. We can define our random variable $X$ as:

$$X \sim \text{Po}(5.4)$$

We want to find the probability that no events occur, which means we are looking for $P(X = 0)$. This falls under **Topic 4: Statistics and Probability** in your syllabus.

steps.title: Select the GDC command
steps.content:
To find the probability of an exact value in a Poisson distribution, we use the **Poisson Probability Density Function** command on the calculator. 

On most calculators, this is labeled as `poissonpdf`. We need to provide two parameters:
1. The mean (often denoted as $\lambda$ or $\mu$): **5.4**
2. The $x$-value we are interested in: **0**

steps.title: Calculate using the GDC
steps.content: Input the values into your calculator to find the result. Remember to round your final answer to 3 significant figures as per IB standards.

gdc.instructions: Go to the Statistics menu, select Distributions, then Poisson, then Poisson PD. Set λ to 5.4 and x to 0.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [VARS] (DISTR), scroll down to find 'poissonpdf('. Enter λ: 5.4 and x value: 0, then select Paste and press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Type the command for the Poisson distribution followed by the PDF function.

calculator.description: Calculate the Poisson probability for X = 0 with a mean of 5.4.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Using the calculator, we find:

$$P(X = 0) \approx 0.00452$$

In an exam, writing down the distribution $X \sim \text{Po}(5.4)$ and the result will earn you the full mark.

parts.content: Calculate the expected number of intervals in a set of 50 intervals with at least 6 events.
parts.markscheme:
$\mathrm{P}(X \geq 6) = 1 - \mathrm{P}(X \leq 5) = 1 - 0.5461... = 0.45386... \approx 0.454$
<marks>M1</marks>

Expected number $= 50 \times 0.45386... \approx 22.7$
<marks>M1</marks><marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Define the Poisson variable
steps.content:
First, we define the random variable $X$ as the number of events in a unit interval. The question states that $X$ follows a Poisson distribution with a mean $(\lambda)$ of 5.4.

$$X \sim \text{Po}(5.4)$$ 

This falls under **Topic 4: Statistics and Probability**, specifically Poisson distributions.

steps.title: Set up the probability
steps.content:
We need to find the probability of having at least 6 events in an interval, which is written as $P(X \geq 6)$. 

Since our graphing calculators typically calculate the cumulative probability from zero up to a value (the CDF), we use the complement rule:

$$P(X \geq 6) = 1 - P(X \leq 5)$$ 

Note that since $X$ must be an integer, "at least 6" is the opposite of "5 or fewer".

steps.title: Calculate probability using GDC
steps.content:
Now, use your calculator's Poisson CDF function with $\lambda = 5.4$ and $x = 5$ to find $P(X \leq 5)$.

$$\begin{aligned} P(X \leq 5) &\approx 0.54613... \\ P(X \geq 6) &= 1 - 0.54613... \\ P(X \geq 6) &\approx 0.45386... \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: In the Statistics menu, go to Distributions -> Poisson -> Poisson CDF. Set λ = 5.4 and x = 5.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [VARS] (DISTR), scroll down to 'poissoncdf('. Enter 5.4 for λ and 5 for x-value.

desmos.instructions: Type the following command into a new cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the cumulative Poisson probability for X ≤ 5 with mean 5.4.

steps.title: Find the expected value
steps.content:
The question asks for the expected number of intervals out of 50 that meet this condition. 

From the **Data Booklet**, we know that for a set of trials, the expected value is calculated by multiplying the number of trials ($n$) by the probability ($p$) of the event occurring in one trial.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Expected number} &= n \times P(X \geq 6) \\ &= 50 \times 0.45386... \\ &\approx 22.7 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the product on the main calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Type '50 * (1 - 0.54613)' on the main screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the multiplication into a new cell.

calculator.description: Multiply the probability by 50 to find the final expected value.

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content: The expected number of intervals is approximately **22.7** (rounded to 3 significant figures).

steps.title: Define the distribution
steps.content:
The number of events $X$ in a unit interval follows a Poisson distribution. From the question, we identify the mean $(\lambda$ or $m)$:

$$\lambda = 5.4$$

We are looking for the probability that an interval has "at least 6" events, written as $\mathrm{P}(X \geq 6)$. This is a discrete distribution, so we can use the complement rule to make our calculation easier.

steps.title: Set up complement probability
steps.content:
To find $\mathrm{P}(X \geq 6)$, we calculate the sum of all probabilities from $X=6$ to infinity. However, it is much faster to subtract the probabilities we *don't* want from the total probability of $1$:

$$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{P}(X \geq 6) &= 1 - \mathrm{P}(X \leq 5) \\ \mathrm{P}(X \geq 6) &= 1 - [\mathrm{P}(X=0) + \mathrm{P}(X=1) + \mathrm{P}(X=2) + \mathrm{P}(X=3) + \mathrm{P}(X=4) + \mathrm{P}(X=5)] \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Apply the Poisson formula
steps.content:
The Poisson probability mass function for the probability of exactly $k$ events is:

$$\mathrm{P}(X = k) = \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^k}{k!}$$

Substituting $\lambda = 5.4$, we calculate each term:

$$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{P}(X=0) &= \frac{e^{-5.4} 5.4^0}{0!} \approx 0.004517 \\ \mathrm{P}(X=1) &= \frac{e^{-5.4} 5.4^1}{1!} \approx 0.024390 \\ \mathrm{P}(X=2) &= \frac{e^{-5.4} 5.4^2}{2!} \approx 0.065852 \\ \mathrm{P}(X=3) &= \frac{e^{-5.4} 5.4^3}{3!} \approx 0.118533 \\ \mathrm{P}(X=4) &= \frac{e^{-5.4} 5.4^4}{4!} \approx 0.160020 \\ \mathrm{P}(X=5) &= \frac{e^{-5.4} 5.4^5}{5!} \approx 0.172821 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Sum and find P(X ≥ 6)
steps.content:
Now, sum these individual probabilities and subtract from $1$:

$$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{P}(X \leq 5) &= 0.004517 + 0.024390 + 0.065852 + 0.118533 + 0.160020 + 0.172821 \\ &= 0.546133 \\ \mathrm{P}(X \geq 6) &= 1 - 0.546133 \\ &= 0.453867 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate expected number
steps.content:
The question asks for the **expected number** of intervals in a set of $50$. We find this by multiplying the total number of intervals ($n$) by the probability of an interval having at least 6 events ($p$):

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Expected Number} &= n \times p \\ &= 50 \times 0.453867 \\ &\approx 22.7 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Go to the Stat/List menu and use the Poisson cumulative distribution function to find P(X ≤ 5), then subtract from 1 and multiply by 50.

ti84.instructions:
1. Multiply 50 by (1 minus the cumulative Poisson probability).
2. Press [2nd] [VARS] (DISTR).
3. Select B: poissoncdf(.
4. Enter λ (μ): 5.4, x value: 5.
5. In the main screen, enter: 50 * (1 - poissoncdf(5.4, 5)).

desmos.instructions: Use the Poisson distribution function to find the cumulative probability from 6 to infinity, then multiply by 50.

calculator.description: Calculate the expected value using the sum of the Poisson probabilities.

parts.content: Using a normal approximation, find the least number of unit intervals $n$ such that the probability of at least 300 events is greater than 0.99. Include a sketch.
parts.markscheme:
Let $Y$ be the number of events in $n$ intervals. $Y \sim \operatorname{Po}(5.4n) \approx \mathrm{N}(5.4n, 5.4n)$.
<marks>M1</marks>

$\mathrm{P}(Y \geq 300) > 0.99 \implies \mathrm{P}(Y > 299.5) > 0.99$ (using continuity correction)
$\mathrm{P}\left(Z > \frac{299.5 - 5.4n}{\sqrt{5.4n}}\right) > 0.99 \implies \frac{299.5 - 5.4n}{\sqrt{5.4n}} < -2.326$
<marks>M1</marks><marks>A1</marks>

Solving for $n$ gives $n \approx 63.4$, so the least integer $n = 64$.
<marks>A1</marks>

Sketch showing a normal distribution curve with the mean $5.4n$ and a shaded region to the right of $299.5$ representing approximately $99\%$ of the area.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/plots/9bb61fea-03f1-46a4-8c3f-be48b7d26ecc.png)
<marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Define the distribution
steps.content:
First, we define the distribution for the total number of events over $n$ intervals. Let $Y$ be the total number of events. 

Since the number of events in one interval follows a Poisson distribution with mean $5.4$, for $n$ intervals, the total mean is $5.4n$. Thus:
$$Y \sim \operatorname{Po}(5.4n)$$

For a normal approximation of a Poisson distribution, we use the property that the mean $(\mu)$ and the variance $(\sigma^2)$ are equal:
$$\mu = 5.4n \quad \text{and} \quad \sigma^2 = 5.4n$$
So, the normal approximation is $Y \approx \mathrm{N}(5.4n, 5.4n)$. This is a key prerequisite from **Topic 4.17/4.18: Poisson and Normal Distributions**.

steps.title: Apply continuity correction
steps.content:
We want the probability of at least 300 events to be greater than 0.99:
$$\mathrm{P}(Y \geq 300) > 0.99$$

Because we are using a continuous normal distribution to approximate a discrete Poisson distribution, we must apply a **continuity correction**. The discrete value 300 covers the interval $[299.5, 300.5]$. To include 300 and everything above it, we start our boundary at 299.5:
$$\mathrm{P}(Y > 299.5) > 0.99$$

highlights.text: at least 300 events is greater than 0.99

steps.title: Standardize to Z-scores
steps.content:
Now we standardize the normal distribution using the Z-score formula. To satisfy the condition, we need to find the critical value $z$ such that $\mathrm{P}(Z > z) = 0.99$.

Using the inverse normal function on your GDC (for a right-tail area of 0.99), we find:
$$z \approx -2.326$$

The standardized version of our probability statement is:
$$\mathrm{P}\left(Z > \frac{299.5 - 5.4n}{\sqrt{5.4n}}\right) > 0.99$$

gdc.instructions: Go to Statistics -> Distributions -> Normal -> Inverse Normal. Set Area to 0.01, μ to 0, and σ to 1.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [VARS] (DISTR), select 3:invNorm(. Enter Area: 0.01 (for left tail), μ: 0, σ: 1.

desmos.instructions: Type `inverseNormal(normaldist(0, 1), 0.01)`

calculator.description: Calculate the critical Z-value for a right-tail area of 0.99 (or left-tail area of 0.01).

steps.title: Set up the inequality
steps.content:
Since the area to the right of $z = -2.326$ is exactly 0.99, to have an area *greater* than 0.99, our standardized boundary must be even further to the left (smaller than $-2.326$).

Setting up the inequality:
$$\frac{299.5 - 5.4n}{\sqrt{5.4n}} < -2.326$$

steps.title: Solve for n
steps.content:
We solve the equation $\frac{299.5 - 5.4n}{\sqrt{5.4n}} = -2.326$ using technology. 

This yields a value of:
$$n \approx 63.435$$

Since $n$ must be an integer and we need the *least* number of intervals such that the probability is *greater* than 0.99, we must round up to ensure the condition is met.
$$n = 64$$

gdc.instructions: In the Solver or Graphing menu, solve the equation (299.5 - 5.4n) / sqrt(5.4n) = -2.326 for n.

ti84.instructions: Press [Y=]. Enter Y1 = (299.5 - 5.4X) / √(5.4X) and Y2 = -2.326. Press [GRAPH] and use [2nd][TRACE] (CALC) -> 5:intersect.

desmos.instructions: Graph y = (299.5 - 5.4x) / sqrt(5.4x) and y = -2.326. Click the intersection point.

calculator.description: Find the intersection or root for n.

steps.title: Draw the sketch
steps.content:
The sketch should show a normal distribution centered at the mean $\mu = 5.4n$. The value $299.5$ is located to the left of the mean. Shading the area to the right of $299.5$ represents the probability $P(Y > 299.5)$, which covers approximately $99\%$ of the total area under the curve.

$$\text{Area} = \mathrm{P}(Y > 299.5) \approx 0.99$$

![Sketch](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/plots/9bb61fea-03f1-46a4-8c3f-be48b7d26ecc.png)

steps.title: Model the distribution
steps.content:
First, we define the random variable $Y$ as the number of events in $n$ unit intervals. Since the number of events in one interval follows a Poisson distribution with mean $5.4$, for $n$ intervals, the mean and variance are both $5.4n$.

$$Y \sim \operatorname{Po}(5.4n)$$

Using the normal approximation (which is appropriate here since $n$ will be large enough for the mean to exceed $15$):

$$Y \approx \mathrm{N}(\mu, \sigma^2) \text{ where } \mu = 5.4n \text{ and } \sigma^2 = 5.4n$$

steps.title: Apply continuity correction
steps.content:
The question asks for the probability of **at least 300 events** to be greater than $0.99$. Because we are approximating a discrete distribution (Poisson) with a continuous one (Normal), we apply a continuity correction. 

$$\mathrm{P}(Y \geq 300) > 0.99 \implies \mathrm{P}(Y > 299.5) > 0.99$$

This means we want the area under the normal curve to the right of $299.5$ to be at least $0.99$.

steps.title: Set up GDC solver
steps.content:
We need to find $n$ such that the probability of the normal distribution being greater than $299.5$ is exactly $0.99$. Since $n$ appears in both the mean ($5.4n$) and the standard deviation ($\sqrt{5.4n}$), we will use the **Numerical Solver** or **Graphing** function on our calculator.

We set up the equation:
$$\text{normCDF}(299.5, \infty, 5.4n, \sqrt{5.4n}) = 0.99$$

gdc.instructions: Go to the Equation Solver. Enter the equation: normCDF(299.5, 1E99, 5.4x, sqrt(5.4x)) = 0.99. Solve for x.

ti84.instructions: Press [MATH] [B:Solver...]. Enter the equation: 0 = normCDF(299.5, 1E99, 5.4X, √(5.4X)) - 0.99. Set a guess for X (e.g., X=60) and press [ALPHA] [SOLVE].

desmos.instructions: Graph y = normaldist(5.4x, sqrt(5.4x)).cdf(299.5, infinity) and y = 0.99. Find the intersection point.

calculator.description: Solve the equation for n using the numerical solver.

steps.title: Solve for n
steps.content:
Using the calculator, we find the value of $n$:

$$n \approx 63.436...$$

Since we need the **least number of intervals** $n$ (where $n$ must be an integer) to ensure the probability is *at least* $0.99$, we must round up to the next whole number.

$$n = 64$$

steps.title: Sketch the distribution
steps.content:
To visualize the result, we sketch the normal distribution. 

1. Draw a bell curve centered at $\mu = 5.4 \times 64 = 345.6$.
2. Mark the boundary $299.5$ to the left of the mean.
3. Shade the region to the right of $299.5$. This shaded area represents the probability $P(Y > 299.5) \approx 0.993$, which is indeed greater than $0.99$.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/plots/9bb61fea-03f1-46a4-8c3f-be48b7d26ecc.png)
